text.title: Interview Scoring, Behavioral Anchors, and Antipatterns
text.summary: Rating scale definition, how to write behavioral anchors for each score, common scoring antipatterns, and metrics for evaluating interview process quality.

rule.statement: Use a 1-5 rating scale; avoid 7- or 10-point scales that introduce false precision without additional behavioral anchor resolution.
rule.rationale: A 1-5 scale maps cleanly to behavioral anchors (exceptional / strong / meets / developing / insufficient). Wider scales force interviewers to make distinctions the anchors cannot support, producing noise rather than signal.

section.title: Scale Definitions
section.p: 5 — Exceptional: far exceeds expectations; demonstrates this competency at a level above the role requirements; would be an outlier contributor on the team.
4 — Strong: exceeds expectations; demonstrates this competency beyond what is minimally required; likely to perform well in the first year without significant ramp.
3 — Meets: meets expectations; demonstrates this competency at the level the role requires; standard hire.
2 — Developing: below expectations; demonstrates partial evidence of this competency; would require significant coaching or favorable conditions.
1 — Insufficient: does not meet requirements; cannot demonstrate this competency; not suitable for this role at this time.

rule.statement: Write behavioral anchors before the interview loop begins, not after scoring; post-hoc anchors are written to justify scores already given.
rule.rationale: Anchors written after scoring rationalize the gut impression rather than defining the evidence standard. Pre-written anchors force the interviewer to look for specific behaviors during the interview, not after.

example.note: Problem-Solving anchor example. Score 5: "Solved an ambiguous, cross-functional problem; created a framework or approach now used by others on the team." Score 4: "Identified root cause through systematic analysis; implemented a solution that persisted beyond the immediate situation." Score 3: "Solved a standard problem using a known approach; logical reasoning but did not go beyond the expected path." Score 2: "Needed significant guidance; could describe the problem but struggled to identify a path forward independently." Score 1: "Could not demonstrate problem-solving ability; responses were hypothetical or described team effort without personal contribution."

antipattern.description: Scoring "culture fit" as an unanchored gut impression.
antipattern.reason: Unanchored "culture fit" is the most common vector for affinity bias. Replace with a specific competency ("alignment with operating principles", "collaboration style") that has behavioral anchors like any other.

antipattern.description: Asking different questions to different candidates and still using a shared scorecard.
antipattern.reason: A scorecard built for Question A cannot validly assess a candidate who answered Question B. The scores are not comparable, and the process will not withstand an audit.

antipattern.description: Discussing candidate impressions in Slack or over lunch before the formal debrief.
antipattern.reason: Informal pre-debrief discussion anchors subsequent scorers to the first opinion shared; it functions as an unstructured debrief with no documentation. Written scores before any discussion is the only protection against anchoring.

antipattern.description: Panel of more than 5 interviewers per candidate.
antipattern.reason: Beyond 5 interviewers, candidate fatigue produces declining response quality in later rounds, and scorecard aggregation yields diminishing returns. Incremental signal from round 6+ is noise, not information.

section.title: Process Quality Metrics
section.p: Interview-to-offer rate: target 15-25%; significantly below may indicate over-filtering, above may indicate insufficient rigor. Quality of hire: percentage of hires meeting or exceeding expectations at 1-year review; target above 80%. Interviewer calibration: score correlation across interviewers on the same candidate; target above 80% agreement within 1 point. Candidate experience: post-interview survey score; target above 4.0/5. Time in interview process: total calendar days from first interview to offer; predictable, not necessarily short, is the target.
